words: SAM SCHRAGER: Tell about your father's coming out to this country,
under what circumstances he came.
LOLA GAMBLE CLYDE: OK. My father was in about the second graduating
class from San Francisco Theological Seminary, which was the only
western Presbyterian seminary in the United States at the time.
And the town of Moscow had aBaptist Church already founded, and
it had a Dunkard Church which met in the homes of the various members.
So agroup of Pennsylvania women who were homesick for the white
churches of their own country, saw the grey-green bunch grass rolling,
and they figured the Presbyterians should have achurch here, too.
So they contacted aDr. Boyd, who was the Presbyterian minister at
Lewiston, and he wrote to San Francisco Theological Seminary. And
as my father was in that spring graduating class, he was given the
mission to come up into Idaho to establish Presbyterian churches. Our
church was the third Presbyterian church founded in the state of Idaho,
as well as the third church in the town of Moscow. Boise had a
Presbyterian church and Lewiston had Presbyterian churches ahead of
. My father always liked to tell how he came by steamboat to the
mouth of the Columbia River and by rlverboat to Walla Walla. He
was subsidised by the great Lad Banking House of Portland. They were
banking and shipping people and immensely wealthy. He got as far as
Walla Walla, here he found anice young horse with abrand new saddle
and bridle waiting for him, and asum of money to come up into this
country. It had all been given by the great La* Company of Boise, and
they were always friends of the Presbyterian Church. They donated $100
toward the building of the first building that was established here,
and when my f.fcr's church at Goldendale, Washington, burned down, they
vere the first people to offer to send money to help with the construetion
of a new church.

speaker: SAM
words: He took the horse and rode out here?

speaker: LC
words: Yes, my father rode up into this country and there were two interesting

speaker: LC
words: stories Iremember him telling. They got as far as Almota. Here he

speaker: LC
words: met agentleman from Scotland. They had three little girls with them, too.

speaker: LC
words: Two of the tiny girls were able to walk, and one was ababy in arms. These

speaker: LC
words: were the Cameron family from . When they found out my father

speaker: LC
words: was a Presbyterian minister, they invited him to come to their tent

speaker: LC
words: south of Moscow and baptise the little girls. This my father did the

speaker: LC
words: following Sunday when he reached Moscow. This was the first Presbyterian

speaker: LC
words: baptismal service conducted in this new territory.

speaker: LC
words: When my father got as far as Lewiston, he was met by Governor

speaker: LC
words: McConnell. He wasn't then governor, but he was one of Moscow's leading

speaker: LC
words: citizens, and together they rode to the top of the Lewiston grade.

speaker: LC
words: Here they stopped at the Ruddy house, which was ahalfway house where

speaker: LC
words: meals were served. There were three lovely young daughters waiting on the

speaker: LC
words: table at the stagecoach stop. Governor McConnell joked (many years later

speaker: LC
words: Iheard the two old men sit and tell this story to each other) how my

speaker: LC
words: father, who was not married at the time, saw these lo^ly young girls

speaker: LC
words: and he told them, "Now girls whenever you get married, be sure you marry

speaker: LC
words: an Irishman." Governor McConnell, who too was Irish, said, "Now, Im

speaker: LC
words: sure this young man is just speaking one word for the Irish and two for

speaker: LC
words: himself." This was agreat joke with the old gentlemen in their old ages.

speaker: SAM
words: When he got to Moscow, what was it that he then had to do to get

speaker: LC
words: Well, there was only one church here at the time and that was the Baptist

speaker: LC
words: Church. They always graciously consented to any other churches coming and

speaker: LC
words: using their building. My father preached there as well as in many of the

speaker: LC
words: little log schoolhouses which had already been established in the rural

speaker: LC
words: communities around. I've heard Papa tell about it being at the Baptist

speaker: LC
words: Church, and he asked for some volunteer, they had alittle reed organ, and

speaker: LC
words: he asked for avolunteer to come and play the organ for the singing. Eovely

speaker: LC
words: young Elizabeth Taylor Clayton stepped forward and played. They played

speaker: LC
words: "The Cross of Christ, Cherish," and my father always loved to sing

speaker: LC
words: that song. That was real interesting how in this pioneer community there

speaker: LC
words: was someone able to play, and they carried on their church services.

speaker: SAM
words: Did he manage to support himself?

speaker: LC
words: Well, he was largely subsidifed. He was sent here by the American Board

speaker: LC
words: of Missions. So those were really missionaries sent out by the

speaker: LC
words: San Francisco Theological Seminary, though beside, just the free will

speaker: LC
words: offering ,Idon't thin^there was really much money in the community

speaker: LC
words: because there weren't over/15 regular members, I imagine, of the church.

speaker: LC
words: But I've also heard my father tell about the first Presbyterian wedding

speaker: LC
words: that he conducted. He was living in a little cabin over at the foot of

speaker: LC
words: the Paradise hill, and he said that he'd noticed avery lovely young

speaker: LC
words: couple that were very interested in religion. Everytime he spoke at any

speaker: LC
words: little schoolhouse, coming riding horseback came this nice looking girl

speaker: LC
words: and her escort. The lovely young girl was a girl named Julia WarnF*1 who

speaker: LC
words: was the adopted daughter of the Bennett Summerfields. Her escort was

speaker: LC
words: handsome, courtly, young George Northrop. They'd listen most attentively

speaker: LC
words: to the sermon. So one lovely October day, the young couple rode

speaker: LC
words: horseback over to where my father's cabin stood. The groom had brought

speaker: LC
words: half asack of hand shelled navy beans. This was to be the wedding fee

speaker: LC
words: that he gave to the minister for performing the marriage ceremony. So,

speaker: LC
words: I've often heard young George Northrop, when he became courtly, old

speaker: LC
words: George Northrop, tell how he'd spent the previous winter hand shelling

speaker: LC
words: that bunch of beans to supply the marriage fee for the wedding.

speaker: SAM
words: Do you think he often took his fee in produce?

speaker: LC
words: Yes, Ithink so. Ithink there was very little money in the country at

speaker: LC
words: that time, and you paid for most of the services in goods, that's right,

speaker: LC
words: in vegetables. But as my father was batching in his pioneer cabin on his

speaker: LC
words: homestead that he had taken up, Iimagine all those things came in pretty

speaker: LC
words: handy.

speaker: SAM
words: What kind of range did he have as far as where he traveled?

speaker: LC
words: Oh, well, I think he went to Colfax because the records show that he

speaker: LC
words: established aPresbyterian Church in the town of Colfax. He also went

speaker: LC
words: down intp the Lapwai country because Iheard him tell how in the spring

speaker: LC
words: freshet the big runoff of the spring down there, he and a friend who

speaker: LC
words: was one of the founders of the Presbyterian church, a man named William

speaker: LC
words: Groat, how they tried to ford the Lapwai Creek it joined the

speaker: LC
words: Clearwater River. The horses got down and floundered,and he walked out

speaker: LC
words: on the tongue of the buggy to cut the tugs loose, but the horses weren't

speaker: LC
words: able to swim and make it to shore. The water carried them away and

speaker: LC
words: drowned them. It was all the two men could do to make it across the

speaker: LC
words: river themselves and save themselves. So he supervised and rode from the

speaker: LC
words: Lapwai country up into this country and over, beyond adoubt, as far as

speaker: LC
words: Colfax. Now, there were no Presbyterian churches in Spokane at that time.

speaker: LC
words: Cowley came a little bit after that, but at that time the radius would

speaker: LC
words: befrom around Moscow.

speaker: SAM
words: Boy, the loss of those horses must have been a great loss to him.

speaker: LC
words: Well, Ithink it was. I imagine they belonged to the ruling elder

speaker: LC
words: of the church, who was Mr. Groat, but it was a loss, anyway. But the

speaker: LC
words: water is high there in the spring, and we've often seen the water way

speaker: LC
words: up over where the little park is now.

speaker: SAM
words: What kind of responsibilities do you think he had as preacher? You said

speaker: SAM
words: he baptised and ...

speaker: LC
words: Oh, yes, and married and conducted the funeral services, as well as

speaker: LC
words: comforting the parishioners and holding regular services. But the church

speaker: LC
words: wasn't built until after my father had gone to Victoria, B. C., so the

speaker: LC
words: church meetings had to be held either in the schoolhouses or in the

speaker: LC
words: Baptist church, which is located right where the present Baptist church

speaker: LC
words: is in Moscow now.

speaker: SAM
words: I have heard that he ministered during a smallpox epidemic?

speaker: LC
words: Well, it was mostldiptheria. There was no toxin, anti-toxin then,and

speaker: LC
words: when the children got diptheria, the little throats just closed up and

speaker: LC
words: they died. There, we had one gentleman in town who had some secret

speaker: LC
words: remedy that was supposed to cure them. It consisted mostly of using coal

speaker: LC
words: oil, and they often called Uncle Jimmy Johnson, "Coal Oil Johnson,"

speaker: LC
words: because this remedy that he had for diptheria combined the use of coal

speaker: LC
words: oil, but I don't know, I rather doubt that that really had any effect

speaker: LC
words: in saving the children. But they certainly died, and the proof of it is

speaker: LC
words: to go through the Moscow cemetery and see all the little headstones there

speaker: LC
words: of children that died in those early years with the Black Diptheria.

speaker: SAM
words: Was your father much involved in fighting the disease?

speaker: LC
words: Well, Idon't think he had much (chuckles) medical ability. Ithink he

speaker: LC
words: was called upon mostly to come and comfort the bereaved and tell them

speaker: LC
words: the Lord giveth and the Lord taketh away; and suffer the little children

speaker: LC
words: to come unto me,and forbid them not for such is the Kingdom of Heaven.

speaker: LC
words: I think all you could do was stand by pretty helplessly and watch them

speaker: LC
words: die. So we've come ajlong way, you know, in preventive medicine since those

speaker: LC
words: early pioneer days.

speaker: SAM
words: Well, when you speak of his preaching do you have much of an idea,

speaker: SAM
words: or can you imagine what his preaching would have been like in those

speaker: SAM
words: day 8?

speaker: LC
words: Well, I think it was pretty orthodox. It was pretty much the old

speaker: LC
words: Presbyterian either saved or you weren't. He wasn't

speaker: LC
words: much of a believer/in predestination, but I think that it stuck

speaker: LC
words: pretty religiously to the Bible and interpreted it pretty literally.

speaker: LC
words: It would be kind of unbending. My father always liked to tell how

speaker: LC
words: he'd studied the exegesis of the thing, and how he'read it in the

speaker: LC
words: Hebrew and the Greek. He did have avery wonderful memory; now this

speaker: LC
words: is something quite interesting, because the old records of the

speaker: LC
words: San Francisco Theological Seminary say that when they gave him his

speaker: LC
words: examination, the examining board marveled at the wonderful memory

speaker: LC
words: he had. Ican remember when Iwas ayoung girl in college,our philosophy

speaker: LC
words: teacher had given me 200 Bible verses, just the chapter and verse, and

speaker: LC
words: Iwas to get aconcordance and look them up and write down what those

speaker: LC
words: verses were. So Ijust fought 1would go home and ask my father how

speaker: LC
words: many of them he knew, and/what he didn't know Icould look up. Out of

speaker: LC
words: the 200 Bible verses ,of course they were well known verses, he knew

speaker: LC
words: all of them and Ididn't have to look up any. So that's how well they

speaker: LC
words: knew the Bible in those days, they knew every word of it. He could recite

speaker: LC
words: whole passages and passages of it and never make amistake. It was

speaker: LC
words: really interesting,and when Iwent back and told my philosophy teacher at

speaker: LC
words: the university what my (chuckles) father had done, that Ididn't have

speaker: LC
words: to look up any of them, that he could give me all of them, he said,

speaker: LC
words: "Well, they don't study, they don't learn the Bible like that anymore,

speaker: LC
words: do they?" And that's right.

speaker: LC
words: Did he work in foreign languages too? Did you say he knew Hebrew?

speaker: LC
words: Well, he had read the Bible in the Hebrew and in the Greek. Idon't

speaker: LC
words: think he ...They were very thorough in the teaching in those days,

speaker: LC
words: they were very, very thorough.

speaker: LC
words: Would he then have spent much of his own time studying when he was out

speaker: LC
words: here, do you think?

speaker: LC
words: Yes, Ithink so. He was agood Bible student, and Ithink he did lots of

speaker: LC
words: certainly trained them well. They were well grounded in the Bible and they weren't

speaker: LC
words: so

speaker: LC
words: concerned with the great social issues of the day as they are now.

speaker: LC
words: But there weren't so many social issues. It was all pioneer country,

speaker: LC
words: you know, and they were mostly concerned with just getting enough

speaker: LC
words: food and clothing and breaking up alittle sod, and getting crops planted.

speaker: LC
words: I think that the Sunday when they went to church was more of a social

speaker: LC
words: event, and it was a chance to get together and a chance to get cleaned

speaker: LC
words: up and get a bath, and go out and meet with some other people. Often

speaker: LC
words: they took their lunches with them and made sort of a picnic out of it

speaker: LC
words: in the good weather. They'd go to the chiich and sing and visit and then

speaker: LC
words: eat, have a nice dinner and then go home. It was the social event of

speaker: LC
words: the week-with many of the pioneer people. I think that that had a real

speaker: LC
words: mission in the lives of the people too. It was areal fine thing.

speaker: SAM
words: Do you have much of an idea of where the Presbyterians in the county

speaker: SAM
words: were drawn from? I mean,were they people who had just been Presbyterians

speaker: SAM
words: back East?

speaker: LC
words: Yes, I think so. I think there were several people around here: a

speaker: LC
words: Mrs. Doak, and aMary Hoke. This was the grandmother of great-grandmother

speaker: LC
words: Hoke. They were all Presbyterians; they were Pennsylvania Dutch. Most

speaker: LC
words: of the Pennsylvania Dutch were from the Dutch Reform from the Presbyterians

speaker: LC
words: the followers of John Calvin from Holland. The Pennsylvania Dutch were

speaker: LC
words: more closely identified with the Presbyterian church than any other church.

speaker: LC
words: Dr. Reeder was one of the ruling elders of the church too. They were of

speaker: LC
words: course of Scotish extraction. And that was real interesting.

speaker: SAM
words: When you say that he didn't believe strongly in predestination, what do

speaker: SAM
words: you mean by that? What was his view?

speaker: LC
words: Well, the old Orthodox Presbyterians believed it was all settled

speaker: LC
words: whether you were going to be damned or if you were going to be saved, and

speaker: LC
words: no matter what you did here on earth, it didn't have much effect. Your

speaker: LC
words: destiny had already been cut out for you. Well, my father didn't hold

speaker: LC
words: with that he thought that we could be saved by the redeeming grace of

speaker: LC
words: Christ, as well as by our own actions, that our own actions would redeem

speaker: LC
words: us. And I think that's abetter belief. Over the yeas the Presbyterians

speaker: LC
words: have certainly rejected all thought of predestination. I think most of

speaker: LC
words: them still believe in the saving grace—that we have been saved by the

speaker: LC
words: death of the Savior, but most of us have taken a much broader view of our

speaker: LC
words: religion than what they had 100 years ago.

speaker: SAM
words: And you do feel that there was very little social teaching as apart

speaker: SAM
words: of the preaching. . .

speaker: LC
words: Well, they always taught about "Thou shalt love they neighbor as thyself

speaker: LC
words: that was reaching out. And the people that were hard pressed for

speaker: LC
words: money, why we should try to help them, we should try to help the poor

speaker: LC
words: and the weak. We were to be good followers of Christ, and of course

speaker: LC
words: alot of the social work of today is founded on the same idea—that if we

speaker: LC
words: would follow the teachings of Jesus we wouldn't need so much emphasis

speaker: LC
words: on social workers and that sort of thing. But,in those days there was

speaker: LC
words: no racial bigotry, you know, of course we never had any of the black

speaker: LC
words: people here. In those times, they felt that the Indians should be savedthat

speaker: LC
words: they had never heard the story of Jesus, and that it was the mission

speaker: LC
words: of the churches and of the Presbyterians to get out and convert them,

speaker: LC
words: while really the Indians had avery fine religion of their own. They

speaker: LC
words: were the first ecologists-they believed in living in harmony with

speaker: LC
words: nature, not to try to conquer nature, but to live in harmony with it.

speaker: LC
words: They said, "The earth is my mother, and the sun is my father." All

speaker: LC
words: nature was their Bible,and they knew every word of it. They saw God

speaker: LC
words: In the setting of the sun, and in the All of it

speaker: LC
words: was the work of God. They said, "The earth is my mother, shall Icut

speaker: LC
words: her hair, or cut hay? Shall Idig under her skin and take out her bones,

speaker: LC
words: like the white people dug the ore from the earth?"They said, "No, we

speaker: LC
words: won't do this because when we die, our mother won't take us back'again

speaker: LC
words: to her breast." So they would just live with what Mother Nature provided

speaker: LC
words: for them,and they wouldn't try to cultivate the land or dig for ore, or

speaker: LC
words: cut the grass or cut the trees. They were the first ecologists-the

speaker: LC
words: Nez Perce Indians.

speaker: SAM
words: The Nez Perce felt this?

speaker: LC
words: Oh yes, uh huh, and Ithink that was avery good religion. Idon't

speaker: LC
words: know how the (chuckles) Presbyterians could improve much on it, really.

speaker: LC
words: Ithink that they had avery good religion of their own, but Ithink

speaker: LC
words: that from the time of Henry Harmon Spalding, all of the Presbyterians tried

speaker: LC
words: to enlighten the good Nez Perce Indians around here.

speaker: LC
words: EARL CLYDE: ... thought you were broadcasting.

speaker: SAM
words: She is.

speaker: LC
words: Oh, yea. (chuckles)

speaker: SAM
words: She's practicing. How are you, Mr. Clyde?

speaker: EC
words: (Chuckles) You've got along waya to go.

speaker: LC
words: (Chuckles) He doesn't like to hear me get started-he thinks Inever

speaker: LC
words: get tfrough. (Chuckles)

speaker: SAM
words: Oh, well there's no end to it.

speaker: LC
words: No no, there's no end to it. (Chuckles)But that's how it was-the way

speaker: LC
words: I see it,anyway.

speaker: SAM
words: Since we're talking about that, what do you think the influence of

speaker: SAM
words: Spalding's bringing religion was to the Nez Perce, to their way of

speaker: SAM
words: looking at things.

speaker: LC
words: Well,the Indians had ,so many things in their favor. They said of

speaker: LC
words: Henry Harmon Spalding and of the Catholics that we Indians may fight

speaker: LC
words: about something that we know something about, but here are people

speaker: LC
words: fighting and they don't know, neither one is sure that they're right.

speaker: LC
words: We never fight about our religion. That seemed very sensible to me

speaker: LC
words: because nobody had the answers, and yet they carried on such arelent

speaker: LC
words: less war against each other. And the Indians didn't believe in that.

speaker: LC
words: Henry Harmon Spalding thought they should only take one wife

speaker: LC
words: some of them had gone along with that and others didn't. But you don't

speaker: LC
words: legislate morals into people,either. Ithink probably the Indians saw

speaker: LC
words: plenty of examples among the fur traders and the early mountain men of

speaker: LC
words: the country that they didn't think they were sinning too much. And I

speaker: LC
words: don't think they were either.

speaker: SAM
words: Do you think that Spalding's effect of bringing the Christian gospel

speaker: SAM
words: to some of the Nez Perce, do you think that had alot to do with

speaker: SAM
words: the dividedness of the tribe, eventually?

speaker: LC
words: Oh yes, Ido, Ithink so. There have always been the Christian and

speaker: LC
words: the non-Christian Indians since the days of Henry Harmon Spacing

speaker: LC
words: cause his first converts were Old Timothy and Old Otef Joseph and

speaker: LC
words: James. The people that folded them, then became mostly Christian.

speaker: LC
words: And the non-Christians were the follower, of the people who resented

speaker: LC
words: the coming of the whites. It went on down to the treaty and non-treaty

speaker: LC
words: Indians. The treaty Indians were the Christian Indians; The non-treaty

speaker: LC
words: Indians were the non-Christians. Ithink it added greatly to it-even

speaker: LC
words: yet they have two summer camps. At will be the Christian Indians,

speaker: LC
words: and around the butte at Mud Springs will be the non-Christian Indians, and

speaker: LC
words: they still call themselves the heathens. There's lot of going back and

speaker: LC
words: forth of course between the two camps, but at one it's mostly religious

speaker: LC
words: education and the singing of hymns, round at the other it1, playing the

speaker: LC
words: tick games and doing alittle gambling, and having alittle fun going

speaker: LC
words: on, alittle drinking, little fun going on. There's still the two camp.

speaker: LC
words: of them. I think that there's still the treaty and the non-treaty Indians,

speaker: LC
words: all right.

speaker: SAM
words: Does this break down to different parts of the reservation? Do

speaker: LC
words: No, they .11 live, they seem to live together. It's not certain

speaker: LC
words: parts, it. just, it's different in the philosophy and different in the

speaker: LC
words: way of life. One of the great gripping chapters of the Nez Perce history

speaker: LC
words: at the great Treaty of 1863. The Treaty of 1855 had given the great

speaker: LC
words: Wallow, country to Old Chief Jo.eph-th.t had alw.y, been hi. homeland.

speaker: LC
words: While he wa. one of Spalding', first Christian converts, his love of

speaker: LC
words: the land surmounted everything else. He wanted to be buried at the foot of

speaker: LC
words: Lake Wallowa, which he is. There's amarker there to mark the place.

speaker: LC
words: But he laid the charge at Young Chief Joseph's door-he said, "You

speaker: LC
words: must never sell the bone, of your father and mother. You must fight for

speaker: LC
words: the beautiful Wallowas." All the tribe, knew that Young Chief Joseph,

speaker: LC
words: who was really apeace-loving man.would dig up the war hatchet and

speaker: LC
words: fight in defense of the beautiful Wallowas. So in the Treaty of 1863,

speaker: LC
words: when they held the great council at Lapwai, at Spalding and Lapwai, into

speaker: LC
words: the tent where sat Howard ani Miles and the rest of them, with their

speaker: LC
words: bras, and their braid, came Old Chief Joseph, with his braids and his

speaker: LC
words: blanket. Under hi. blanket he carried the white man's covenant, the

speaker: LC
words: white man's promise of Christianity and of brotherhood and fair play.

speaker: LC
words: It wa. the Book of Maew-the little go.pel that Henry Harmon Spa Iding

speaker: LC
words: had given him off the first printing press. A. he sat there and listened,

speaker: LC
words: it gradually dawned on him that the new treaty intended to take away,

speaker: LC
words: the beautiful Wallowas from him, and that he and all hi. followers were

speaker: LC
words: going to be put on areservation. So the old man arose from the table

speaker: LC
words: and from under hi, blanket he drew out the Book of MaAew. He grabbed

speaker: LC
words: the page, out of the book and tore them into thousands of pieces and

speaker: LC
words: threw them in the face, of the generals assembled there at the table,

speaker: LC
words: and walked out. Henry Harmon Spalding wrote in his diary, he said,

speaker: LC
words: "Old Chief Joseph has gone back to Egypt." But he much preferred the

speaker: LC
words: heathens to the white man's civilization with their false promises

speaker: LC
words: of brotherhood and fair play and equality. That's one of the great

speaker: LC
words: gripping moints of history, all right, when he saw that all the promise,

speaker: LC
words: were ju.t nothing but ascrap of paper, and the Wallowas were going to

speaker: LC
words: be lost to him after all.

speaker: LC
words: (End of Side A)

speaker: SAM
words: Since we were talking about the Spa Idings, alittle bit of what you

speaker: SAM
words: were telling me about Eliza Spalding, what was her role in the under

speaker: SAM
words: taking of

speaker: LC
words: in her own right. She was a studious woman, and abright girl, and was

speaker: LC
words: woman of great patience; otherwise she couldn't have gotten along with

speaker: LC
words: old Henry Harmon Spalding. Always she knew that she was not the pretty

speaker: LC
words: one, but she did know that pretty is as pretty does, and she certainly

speaker: LC
words: did very, very prettily. When she married Henry Harmon Spa lding after

speaker: LC
words: he had been rejected by Narcissa Prentiss Whitman because of his illegiti

speaker: LC
words: mate birth, she married him and educated him. He'd always had quite an

speaker: LC
words: inferiority complex because,in those days, nowadays we know it's just

speaker: LC
words: illegitimate parents and we put no stigma on it, in those days it was

speaker: LC
words: a great crime to be an illegitimate child. So she went with him to the

speaker: LC
words: great Lane Theological Seminary, and here they studied under the great
